label: Uneasy calm
summary:
Sunday turned on a single question: would the Iran war actually end? President Trump posted that 'The Deal is scheduled to get signed tomorrow, and immediately after it is signed, the Hormuz Strait is OPEN TO ALL,' insisting Iran 'no longer want a Nuclear Weapon, nor will they have one' and that no money would change hands. Pakistan's prime minister Shehbaz Sharif, mediating, said a pact was closer 'than ever before' and could be finalized 'in the next 24 hours,' with C-17s pre-positioning equipment in Europe for a possible Geneva signing attended by VP JD Vance. But the caveats stayed heavy: Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei flatly denied a June 14 ceremony and said 'nothing has been finalised,' senior Israeli officials warned the emerging terms 'endanger Israel's security interests,' Netanyahu convened his security cabinet, and Hezbollah had just struck a military zone in northern Israel. So this is hope edging toward a deal, not yet peace, two days before the G7 gathers at Évian in the French Alps with Iran and Ukraine atop the agenda.

The wider ledger stayed mixed. Friday's markets had closed solidly higher (Dow +0.70%, S&P 500 +0.5%, Nasdaq +0.3%) with oil easing toward $85 after SpaceX's strong Nasdaq debut, reading the de-escalation as relief. Recovery from the magnitude-7.8 Mindanao earthquake ground on — the toll near 47-53 dead, 600-plus injured and some 433,000 people affected, with whole municipalities still cut off — while Sudan's El Fasher famine and genocide and Russia's overnight strikes on Ukraine (8 killed, 62 injured) kept the floor low. Against that, brighter notes accumulated: Northern Ireland's anti-immigrant riots subsided as up to 20,000 marched against racism in Belfast and Derry, an AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine cleared its first human trial, a France-sized marine reserve was confirmed around French Polynesia, mangrove forests logged a net gain, and U.S. solar out-generated coal for the first month ever. (Scored on the day's facts: a 'sign tomorrow' announcement and steady markets lift the day to the upper edge of the 'mixed' band, but a contested, unsigned deal, Israeli alarm, a deadly quake aftermath and ongoing famine keep it short of 'good.')

drivers.headline: Trump says the U.S.-Iran deal to end the war is 'scheduled to get signed tomorrow,' with the Strait of Hormuz to open 'to all' immediately and Pakistan's PM saying a pact could be finalized within 24 hours

Iran's foreign ministry denies any June 14 signing ceremony and says 'nothing has been finalised,' while senior Israeli officials warn the emerging terms 'endanger Israel's security interests' and Netanyahu convenes his security cabinet
U.S. stocks close higher (Dow +0.70%, S&P 500 +0.5%, Nasdaq +0.3%) as oil eases toward $85 on Iran de-escalation hopes and SpaceX surges 19% in a strong Nasdaq debut
Russian overnight drone and missile strikes kill 8 and injure 62 across Ukraine, hitting a market in Dnipropetrovsk Oblast, as the war grinds into another summer of attrition
Recovery from the magnitude-7.8 Mindanao earthquake grinds on with roughly 47-53 dead, more than 600 injured and some 433,000 people affected, as whole municipalities remain cut off by landslides
Famine and genocide persist in Sudan's El Fasher and North Darfur, where the UN says some 2 million people face famine and more than half of children under five suffer acute malnutrition
Northern Ireland's anti-immigrant riots subside as thousands - organizers claim up to 20,000 - march against racism in Belfast and Derry under banners reading 'Riots don't speak for Belfast'

An AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine clears its first human trial, proving safe and well-tolerated and generating immune responses against SARS-CoV-2, the original SARS virus and related bat coronaviruses
A France-sized marine reserve of about 200,000 square miles is confirmed around French Polynesia's Austral and Marquesas islands, barring mining, trawling and industrial fishing
U.S. solar out-generates coal for the first time in a single month - 12.8% of electricity in May against coal's 12.2% - and global offshore wind capacity is set to quadruple by 2035
Researchers identify 14 blood-protein signatures that can predict lung-cancer risk years in advance, drawn from a study of more than 48,000 UK participants
Global mangrove forests log a net gain over 16 years as conservation reverses decades of loss, in what scientists call 'a rare conservation success story'

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (2026 Strait of Hormuz crisis / 2026 Mindanao earthquake / 2026 Northern Ireland riots / 52nd G7 summit)
